candidate_problems.text: Is the conjecture's core identification—'entropy increase is the physical form of record creation and record dispersal'—actually valid in a precise, general sense? The main open problem is to formulate a rigorous measure of 'records,' 'legibility,' and 'memory' that can be related to thermodynamic entropy, mutual information, coarse-graining, and decoherence, and to determine when record accumulation tracks entropy increase versus when they come apart.

Does the conjecture explain the arrow of time, or merely redescribe the Past Hypothesis in informational language? The unresolved tension is whether 'memory/record architecture' is an independent explanatory principle, or whether it itself requires the same low-entropy boundary condition it is supposed to illuminate. A worthwhile problem is to identify whether durable records can arise generically from time-symmetric laws, or only given highly special initial conditions.

Why are records asymmetrically readable only toward the past if the microdynamics are time-reversal symmetric? The open question is to explain, without circularity, why later states contain robust, redundant, accessible traces of earlier states, while earlier states do not contain equally accessible traces of later states. This invites a deeper investigation of retrodiction vs prediction, causal asymmetry, and whether observability/redundancy of records can be derived from dynamics plus boundary conditions rather than assumed.
